label: Adeles
description: The adele ring $\mathbb{A}_K$ of a number field $K$ is the restricted product of all completions $K_v$ (archimedean and non-archimedean), encoding local-global arithmetic simultaneously. It is a locally compact topological ring central to modern number theory and automorphic forms.

label: Algebraic K-theory
description: Algebraic K-theory assigns a sequence of abelian groups $K_n(R)$ to a ring $R$, generalising classical invariants such as the Grothendieck group $K_0$ and the Whitehead group $K_1$. It connects algebraic topology, number theory, and geometric topology via trace maps and assembly maps.

label: Algebraic topology
description: Algebraic topology studies topological spaces by associating algebraic invariants — groups, rings, modules — that are preserved under homeomorphism or homotopy equivalence. Core tools include homology, cohomology, homotopy groups, and spectral sequences.

label: Amenable group
description: A locally compact group $G$ is amenable if it admits a left-invariant mean on $L^\infty(G)$, equivalently if it satisfies the Følner condition. Amenability governs fixed-point properties, operator-algebraic approximation, and the Baum–Connes conjecture.

label: Assembly map
description: The assembly map $\mu\colon K_*^G(\underline{E}G) \to K_*(C^*_r G)$ relates the equivariant K-homology of the classifying space for proper actions to the K-theory of the reduced group C*-algebra. It is the central object in the Baum–Connes and Farrell–Jones conjectures.

label: Atiyah–Singer index theorem
description: The Atiyah–Singer index theorem equates the analytical index of an elliptic differential operator $D$ on a compact manifold with a topological index computed from characteristic classes. It unifies the Gauss–Bonnet theorem, Hirzebruch signature theorem, and Riemann–Roch theorem as special cases.

label: Automorphic form
description: An automorphic form is a smooth function on $G(\mathbb{A})$ (for a reductive group $G$ over a number field) that is left $G(K)$-invariant and satisfies growth and Hecke-equivariance conditions. Classical modular forms are the $GL_2$ case; the Langlands programme organises automorphic forms for gener…

label: Baum–Connes conjecture
description: The Baum–Connes conjecture asserts that the assembly map $\mu\colon K_*^G(\underline{E}G) \to K_*(C^*_r G)$ is an isomorphism for every locally compact group $G$. It implies the Novikov conjecture and the Kadison–Kaplansky idempotent conjecture as consequences.

label: Bers embedding
description: The Bers embedding realises the Teichmüller space $\mathcal{T}(S)$ as a bounded domain in the space of holomorphic quadratic differentials on the Riemann surface $\bar S$, providing a complex-analytic structure on $\mathcal{T}(S)$. It is a key tool in the complex geometry of moduli spaces of Riemann…

label: BMO (Bounded Mean Oscillation)
description: A function $f \in BMO(\mathbb{R}^n)$ if $\sup_Q \frac{1}{|Q|}\int_Q |f - f_Q|\,dx < \infty$ over all cubes $Q$. BMO is the natural endpoint space for Calderón–Zygmund operators and is dual to the Hardy space $H^1$.

label: Borel conjecture
description: The Borel conjecture states that two closed aspherical manifolds with isomorphic fundamental groups are homeomorphic. It follows from the Farrell–Jones conjecture and is an analogue of Mostow rigidity for non-positively curved manifolds.

label: BPS state
description: BPS states are field-theory or string-theory states that saturate the Bogomolny–Prasad–Sommerfield bound, preserving a fraction of supersymmetry. Their counts are related to Donaldson–Thomas invariants and motivic generating series in enumerative geometry.

label: Brownian motion
description: Brownian motion $\{B_t\}_{t\ge 0}$ is the canonical continuous-path Gaussian process with independent stationary increments $B_t - B_s \sim \mathcal{N}(0,t-s)$. It underpins stochastic calculus, parabolic PDEs via the Feynman–Kac formula, and the continuum limits of random discrete structures.

label: Calderón–Zygmund theory
description: Calderón–Zygmund theory provides $L^p$ boundedness ($`1 < p < \infty`$) and weak-type $(1,1)$ estimates for singular integral operators of the form $Tf(x) = \int K(x,y)f(y)\,dy$ with kernel satisfying size and smoothness conditions. It is the backbone of modern harmonic analysis and elliptic PDE reg…

label: Cap product
description: The cap product $\frown\colon H^p(X;R)\otimes H_n(X;R)\to H_{n-p}(X;R)$ pairs a cohomology class with a homology class to yield a lower-dimensional homology class. It is used to formulate Poincaré duality and appears naturally in assembly-map constructions.

label: Characteristic class
description: Characteristic classes are cohomological invariants of vector bundles: Chern classes $c_i(E)\in H^{2i}(X;\mathbb{Z})$, Pontryagin classes $p_i(E)\in H^{4i}(X;\mathbb{Z})$, and the Euler class. They appear in the Atiyah–Singer index theorem and in the study of manifold topology.

label: Classifying space
description: The classifying space $BG$ of a topological group $G$ is the base of the universal principal $G$-bundle $EG \to BG$; homotopy classes of maps $X \to BG$ classify principal $G$-bundles over $X$. For discrete groups, $BG = K(G,1)$ is an Eilenberg–Mac Lane space with $\pi_1 = G$.

label: Cobordism
description: Two closed $n$-manifolds $M$, $N$ are cobordant if there exists a compact $(n+1)$-manifold $W$ with $\partial W = M \sqcup N$. Cobordism rings, both oriented and unoriented, are computed by Thom's theorem and play a role in surgery theory and topological field theories.

label: Cohomological dimension
description: The cohomological dimension $\mathrm{cd}(G)$ of a group $G$ is the smallest $n$ such that $H^k(G;M)=0$ for all $k>n$ and all $G$-modules $M$. It bounds the geometric dimension of the classifying space $BG$ and features in finiteness conditions for groups.

label: Coarse geometry
description: Coarse geometry studies metric spaces up to quasi-isometry and large-scale equivalence, ignoring local structure. The coarse Baum–Connes conjecture, formulated in this setting, implies injectivity of the assembly map for groups with finite asymptotic dimension.

label: Conformal field theory
description: A conformal field theory (CFT) is a quantum field theory invariant under conformal transformations (angle-preserving maps). In two dimensions, the infinite-dimensional Virasoro algebra governs the structure; CFTs arise in string theory, statistical mechanics at criticality, and via the AdS/CFT corre…

label: Continuum limit
description: A continuum limit is the process of taking a sequence of discrete models (lattice, graph, combinatorial structure) whose mesh size tends to zero and extracting a limiting continuous object. In probability, Brownian motion and SLE curves arise as continuum limits of random walks and interfaces.

label: Controlled topology
description: Controlled topology studies maps and homotopies that are "small" with respect to a given control map to a metric space, providing a technical framework for surgery and h-cobordism theorems in the non-simply-connected setting. It underpins the proof of the Farrell–Jones conjecture via transfer maps.

label: Curvature
description: Curvature measures the deviation of a geometric object from being flat: sectional curvature $K(\sigma)$ for Riemannian manifolds, Ricci curvature $\mathrm{Ric}$, and scalar curvature $R = \mathrm{tr}(\mathrm{Ric})$. Sign conditions on curvature control topology (sphere theorem, Cheeger–Gromoll split…

label: Deligne–Mumford stack
description: A Deligne–Mumford stack is an algebraic stack whose diagonal is representable and unramified, generalising schemes to allow finite automorphism groups at each point. The moduli stack $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,n}$ of stable $n$-pointed genus-$g$ curves is the prototypical example.

label: Derived category
description: The derived category $D(\mathcal{A})$ of an abelian category $\mathcal{A}$ is obtained by formally inverting quasi-isomorphisms in the category of chain complexes. It provides the natural setting for derived functors, sheaf cohomology, and Fourier–Mukai transforms in algebraic geometry.

label: Donaldson–Thomas invariant
description: Donaldson–Thomas (DT) invariants count (in a virtual sense) sheaves or ideal sheaves on a Calabi–Yau threefold, encoding curve-counting data. They are related to Gromov–Witten invariants by the MNOP conjecture and to BPS state counts in string theory.

label: Derived algebraic geometry
description: Derived algebraic geometry replaces commutative rings with simplicial commutative rings or $E_\infty$-ring spectra, allowing deformation theory and intersection theory to work without excess dimension hypotheses. It provides the correct framework for shifted symplectic structures and virtual fundame…

label: Dirichlet form
description: A Dirichlet form is a densely defined, closed, non-negative symmetric bilinear form $\mathcal{E}$ on $L^2(\mu)$ satisfying the Markov property (the unit contraction operates). It provides an analytic counterpart to symmetric Markov processes and extends diffusion theory to fractals and metric measur…

label: Donaldson invariant
description: Donaldson invariants are smooth 4-manifold invariants defined by counting anti-self-dual Yang–Mills connections (instantons) on principal $SU(2)$-bundles. They distinguished homeomorphic but non-diffeomorphic smooth 4-manifolds before Seiberg–Witten theory.

label: Eilenberg–Mac Lane space
description: An Eilenberg–Mac Lane space $K(G,n)$ is a CW-complex with $\pi_n = G$ and all other homotopy groups trivial. These spaces represent ordinary cohomology: $[X, K(G,n)] \cong H^n(X;G)$, and they are the building blocks of the Postnikov tower.

label: Elliptic operator
description: A linear partial differential operator $P$ of order $m$ is elliptic if its principal symbol $\sigma_m(P)(x,\xi)$ is invertible for all $\xi \neq 0$. Elliptic operators on compact manifolds have finite-dimensional kernels and cokernels, making the index well-defined.

label: Enumerative geometry
description: Enumerative geometry counts geometric objects (curves, surfaces, sheaves) satisfying prescribed conditions, typically producing integers or rational numbers. Modern techniques — Gromov–Witten theory, Donaldson–Thomas theory, motivic integration — place these counts in cohomological or categorical fr…

label: Ergodic theory
description: Ergodic theory studies measure-preserving dynamical systems and the statistical behaviour of orbits. The ergodic theorem of Birkhoff asserts that time averages equal space averages for ergodic systems; connections to operator algebras arise via the von Neumann mean ergodic theorem.

label: Exotic sphere
description: An exotic sphere is a smooth manifold homeomorphic but not diffeomorphic to the standard $S^n$. Milnor constructed the first examples on $S^7$ in 1956; their classification uses surgery theory and is related to the image of the J-homomorphism in stable homotopy theory.

label: Farrell–Jones conjecture
description: The Farrell–Jones conjecture asserts that the assembly map $H_*^G(\mathcal{E}_{\mathcal{VC}}G; \mathbf{K}_R) \to K_*(RG)$ (and its L-theory analogue) is an isomorphism for every group $G$ and every ring $R$. It implies the Borel and Novikov conjectures for torsion-free groups.

label: Floer homology
description: Floer homology is a family of infinite-dimensional Morse theories: instanton Floer homology counts anti-self-dual connections on 4-manifolds with boundary; Heegaard Floer and monopole Floer homologies are 3-manifold invariants. They categorify classical invariants and appear in mirror symmetry.

label: Functor of points
description: In algebraic geometry, a scheme $X$ is understood via its functor of points $h_X\colon \mathbf{Sch}^\mathrm{op}\to \mathbf{Set}$, $S\mapsto X(S) = \mathrm{Hom}(S,X)$. This perspective generalises to algebraic stacks and derived geometry by replacing $\mathbf{Set}$ with groupoids or $\infty$-groupoid…

label: Fundamental group
description: The fundamental group $\pi_1(X,x_0)$ classifies loops in $X$ based at $x_0$ up to homotopy, with composition given by concatenation. It is the first in the sequence of homotopy groups and governs covering space theory and the van Kampen theorem.

label: Γ-convergence
description: $\Gamma$-convergence is a notion of variational convergence for functionals $F_\varepsilon \colon X \to \overline{\mathbb{R}}$ such that minimisers of $F_\varepsilon$ converge to minimisers of the $\Gamma$-limit $F$. It is the standard tool for deriving effective energies in homogenisation and phase…

label: Geometric group theory
description: Geometric group theory studies groups via their actions on metric spaces, treating a finitely generated group $G$ as a metric space via the word metric on a Cayley graph. Key themes include quasi-isometry invariants, hyperbolic groups, and CAT(0) cube complexes.

label: Geometric measure theory
description: Geometric measure theory (GMT) extends classical differential geometry to non-smooth objects — rectifiable sets, currents, varifolds — using measure-theoretic tools. It provides existence and regularity theory for area-minimising surfaces and is applied in minimal surface theory and shape analysis.

label: Gromov–Hausdorff convergence
description: A sequence of metric spaces $(X_n, d_n)$ converges in the Gromov–Hausdorff sense to $(X,d)$ if they can be isometrically embedded into a common metric space such that $X_n$ converges to $X$ in the Hausdorff metric. This is the natural topology for studying limits of Riemannian manifolds with curvatu…

label: Gromov–Witten invariant
description: Gromov–Witten invariants count (in a virtual sense) pseudo-holomorphic curves of genus $g$ and degree $\beta$ in a symplectic manifold $X$, defined via integration over the moduli space $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,n}(X,\beta)$. They encode quantum corrections to the cohomology ring, yielding the quan…

label: Grove programme
description: The Grove programme seeks to classify Riemannian manifolds satisfying geometric bounds (lower curvature, upper diameter) up to diffeomorphism or homeomorphism. It uses comparison geometry, Alexandrov spaces, and equivariant techniques.

label: Haagerup property
description: A locally compact group $G$ has the Haagerup property (a-T-menability) if it admits a metrically proper continuous action by affine isometries on a Hilbert space. Groups with this property satisfy the Baum–Connes conjecture with coefficients; examples include amenable groups and $\mathrm{SL}(2,\math…

label: Hardy space
description: The Hardy space $H^p$ ($`0 < p < \infty`$) on the upper half-plane or disk consists of holomorphic functions whose $L^p$ means over horizontal lines (or circles) are uniformly bounded. Real Hardy spaces $H^p(\mathbb{R}^n)$ for $p \le 1$ replace $L^p$ as the natural domain for singular integrals when…

label: Harmonic analysis
description: Harmonic analysis studies the representation of functions and signals via superposition of basic waves, from classical Fourier series on the circle to abstract harmonic analysis on locally compact groups. Modern harmonic analysis encompasses Calderón–Zygmund theory, time-frequency analysis, and wave…

label: Harmonic map
description: A smooth map $f\colon (M,g)\to (N,h)$ between Riemannian manifolds is harmonic if it is a critical point of the Dirichlet energy $E(f)=\int_M |df|^2\,\mathrm{vol}_g$, equivalently if its tension field $\tau(f)=\mathrm{tr}\nabla df$ vanishes. Harmonic maps include geodesics, minimal immersions, and h…

label: Higher category theory
description: Higher category theory extends ordinary category theory to $n$-categories and $\infty$-categories, where morphisms exist between morphisms at every level. The $(\infty,1)$-categorical framework (quasi-categories, complete Segal spaces) underlies derived algebraic geometry and modern homotopy theory.

label: Homogenisation
description:
Homogenisation is the process of deriving effective macroscopic equations from rapidly oscillating heterogeneous media, replacing $-
abla\cdot(A(x/\varepsilon)
abla u_\varepsilon)=f$ with an effective equation $-
abla\cdot(A^*
abla u)=f$ as $\varepsilon	o 0$. It relies on $\Gamma$-convergence a…

label: Homotopy-coherent diagram
description: A homotopy-coherent diagram is a functor from an indexing category into an $\infty$-category (or, classically, a diagram in a model category satisfying coherence conditions up to homotopy). It replaces strict commutativity with higher homotopies, essential in derived and spectral settings.

label: Homotopy group
description: The $n$-th homotopy group $\pi_n(X,x_0) = [(S^n, *),(X,x_0)]$ classifies continuous maps from $S^n$ to $X$ up to based homotopy, with group structure from the co-group structure on $S^n$. Stable homotopy groups $\pi_n^s = \varinjlim_k \pi_{n+k}(S^k)$ are the coefficients of the sphere spectrum.

label: Homotopy type theory
description: Homotopy type theory (HoTT) interprets types as spaces and equalities as paths, making Martin-Löf type theory into a language for $\infty$-groupoids. The univalence axiom asserts that equivalent types are equal, providing a foundation for mathematics in which isomorphic structures are identified.

label: Hyperbolic group
description: A finitely generated group $G$ is hyperbolic in the sense of Gromov if its Cayley graph is a $\delta$-hyperbolic metric space: every geodesic triangle is $\delta$-slim. Hyperbolic groups have solvable word and conjugacy problems and satisfy the Baum–Connes and Farrell–Jones conjectures.

label: ∞-category
description: An $\infty$-category (quasi-category) is a simplicial set satisfying the inner horn-filling condition, generalising both categories ($n \le 1$ truncated) and Kan complexes (groupoids). Lurie's theory of $(\infty,1)$-categories provides a foundation for derived algebraic geometry and higher topos the…

label: Index theory
description: Index theory computes the Fredholm index $\mathrm{ind}(D) = \dim\ker D - \dim\ker D^*$ of an elliptic operator $D$ in terms of topological data. The Atiyah–Singer index theorem, its equivariant and families versions, and the coarse index are central results.

label: Intersection homology
description: Intersection homology $IH^*_{\bar p}(X)$ is a homology theory for singular spaces (stratified pseudomanifolds) that satisfies Poincaré duality by imposing perversity conditions $\bar p$ on how chains may intersect the singular locus. It agrees with ordinary homology on manifolds and underlies the de…

label: J-homomorphism
description: The J-homomorphism $J\colon \pi_n(SO) \to \pi_n^s$ maps the homotopy groups of the stable orthogonal group to the stable homotopy groups of spheres, detecting elements arising from the action of $SO(n)$ on $S^{n-1}$. Its image is related to Bernoulli numbers and exotic spheres.

label: K-theory
description: K-theory assigns abelian groups to rings, spaces, or C*-algebras measuring vector bundles or projective modules. Topological K-theory $K^*(X)$ and algebraic K-theory $K_*(R)$ are connected by the Atiyah–Hirzebruch spectral sequence and are central to index theory and the classification of C*-algebra…

label: Kähler manifold
description: A Kähler manifold is a complex manifold $(M, J)$ equipped with a Riemannian metric $g$ compatible with $J$ such that the associated $(1,1)$-form $\omega = g(J\cdot,\cdot)$ is closed. Kähler geometry combines complex, Riemannian, and symplectic structures; examples include $\mathbb{CP}^n$ and smooth …

label: Kasparov KK-theory
description: Kasparov's bivariant KK-theory $KK(A,B)$ is a bifunctor on C*-algebras equipped with a Kasparov product, unifying K-theory, K-homology, and index theory in a single framework. It is the algebraic engine behind the Baum–Connes conjecture and Poincaré duality for C*-algebras.

label: L-theory
description: Algebraic L-theory $L_*(R)$ is the quadratic form analogue of algebraic K-theory, built from quadratic modules over a ring with involution $R$. It classifies surgery obstructions for manifolds and appears in the L-theory assembly map of the Farrell–Jones conjecture.

label: Langlands programme
description: The Langlands programme is a web of far-reaching conjectures relating automorphic representations of reductive groups over number fields or function fields to Galois representations (and more generally to motives). The geometric Langlands programme reformulates these conjectures in terms of D-module…

label: Locally compact group
description: A locally compact group is a topological group whose underlying space is locally compact and Hausdorff, admitting a (essentially unique) Haar measure. Key examples are Lie groups, $p$-adic groups, and adelic groups; their unitary representation theory is governed by the Plancherel theorem.

label: Mapping class group
description: The mapping class group $\mathrm{Mod}(S)$ of a surface $S$ is the group of isotopy classes of orientation-preserving homeomorphisms $S \to S$. It acts properly discontinuously on Teichmüller space, with quotient the moduli space $\mathcal{M}_g$; it has connections to braid groups and quantum topolog…

label: Mean curvature flow
description: Mean curvature flow evolves a hypersurface $\Sigma_t \subset \mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ by the equation $\partial_t x = H\nu$, where $H$ is the mean curvature and $\nu$ the unit normal. Singularities form in finite time; the flow is studied using monotonicity formulas, blow-up analysis, and level-set methods…

label: Minimal surface
description: A minimal surface is a surface of vanishing mean curvature, equivalently a critical point of the area functional. Classical examples include the catenoid and helicoid; existence and regularity are studied via the Plateau problem, geometric measure theory, and PDE methods.

label: Model category
description: A model category is a category equipped with three distinguished classes of morphisms — weak equivalences, fibrations, cofibrations — satisfying Quillen's axioms, providing an abstract framework for homotopy theory. The homotopy category is obtained by formally inverting weak equivalences.

label: Moduli space
description: A moduli space parameterises a class of geometric objects up to isomorphism; for example, $\mathcal{M}_g$ parameterises compact Riemann surfaces of genus $g$ and $\mathcal{M}_{g,n}$ adds $n$ marked points. Moduli spaces are studied via deformation theory, GIT quotients, and algebraic stacks.

label: Motivic cohomology
description: Motivic cohomology $H^{p,q}(X,\mathbb{Z})$ is a bigraded cohomology theory for algebraic varieties, constructed via algebraic cycles (Bloch's higher Chow groups). It maps to étale cohomology (Beilinson–Lichtenbaum), singular cohomology, and algebraic K-theory via the motivic spectral sequence.

label: Multiscale phenomena
description: Multiscale phenomena involve coupled dynamics or geometry across widely separated length or time scales, requiring methods that bridge micro and macro descriptions. Mathematical tools include homogenisation, renormalisation group analysis, and heterogeneous multiscale methods.

label: Non-commutative geometry
description: Connes's non-commutative geometry replaces a classical space by the spectral data of a Dirac-type operator on a Hilbert space module over a (possibly non-commutative) C*-algebra, recovering geometric invariants via cyclic cohomology and the local index formula. It provides a geometric framework for …

label: Novikov conjecture
description: The Novikov conjecture asserts that higher signatures $\langle \mathcal{L}(M)\cup f^*\alpha,[M]\rangle$ are homotopy invariants of closed oriented manifolds, for any cohomology class $\alpha \in H^*(B\pi_1(M);\mathbb{Q})$. It follows from the rational injectivity of the assembly map and from the Far…

label: Operator algebra
description: An operator algebra is an algebra of bounded (or unbounded) linear operators on a Hilbert space, closed in some operator topology. The two main classes are C*-algebras (norm-closed) and von Neumann algebras (weakly closed); they are central to quantum mechanics, ergodic theory, and non-commutative g…

label: $p$-adic numbers
description: The field $\mathbb{Q}_p$ of $p$-adic numbers is the completion of $\mathbb{Q}$ with respect to the $p$-adic absolute value $|x|_p = p^{-v_p(x)}$, where $v_p$ is the $p$-adic valuation. The $p$-adic integers $\mathbb{Z}_p$ form a compact open subgroup; $\mathbb{Q}_p$ is a local field fundamental to n…

label: Perverse sheaf
description: A perverse sheaf on a stratified algebraic variety is a complex of constructible sheaves satisfying support and co-support conditions determined by the perversity; the category of perverse sheaves is the heart of the perverse t-structure on the derived category. The decomposition theorem states that…

label: Poincaré duality
description: For a closed oriented $n$-manifold $M$, Poincaré duality gives an isomorphism $H^k(M;R) \cong H_{n-k}(M;R)$ induced by capping with the fundamental class $[M]$. It generalises to Poincaré duality spaces, intersection homology for singular spaces, and Poincaré duality algebras in the derived setting.

label: Probabilistic combinatorics
description: Probabilistic combinatorics uses probability theory — random graphs, the probabilistic method — to prove existence results and analyse typical behaviour of combinatorial structures. Erdős and Rényi's random graph $G(n,p)$ and the probabilistic method are foundational.

label: Quantum field theory
description: A quantum field theory (QFT) assigns operator-valued distributions (fields) to spacetime, with dynamics governed by a Lagrangian; correlation functions are the primary observables. Rigorous mathematical formulations include Wightman axioms, Haag–Kastler nets, and functorial QFT via cobordism categor…

label: Quasi-isometry
description: A quasi-isometry between metric spaces is a map $f\colon X\to Y$ such that $\frac{1}{C}d(x,x')-C \le d(f(x),f(x'))\le Cd(x,x')+C$ and every point of $Y$ lies within $C$ of the image, for some $C>0$. Quasi-isometry is the equivalence relation of geometric group theory, preserving properties like hype…

label: Random matrix theory
description: Random matrix theory studies the spectral statistics of matrices with random entries (GUE, GOE, GUE ensembles). Universality results show that eigenvalue spacing distributions — governed by the Wigner semicircle law and GUE/GOE kernels — appear across diverse systems from nuclear physics to number t…

label: Regularity theory
description: Regularity theory in PDEs studies how much smoothness solutions inherit from the data and the operator. Schauder estimates give $C^{k,\alpha}$ regularity for elliptic equations with Hölder coefficients; De Giorgi–Nash–Moser theory gives $C^{0,\alpha}$ bounds for divergence-form equations with only $…

label: Riemannian geometry
description: Riemannian geometry studies smooth manifolds equipped with a positive-definite inner product $g_p$ on each tangent space, varying smoothly in $p$. It provides the notions of length, angle, geodesic, curvature, and volume that underpin general relativity, comparison geometry, and geometric analysis.

label: Rough paths
description: Rough path theory, due to Lyons, provides a deterministic framework for integrating and solving differential equations driven by irregular signals (paths of Hölder regularity $\alpha < 1/2$) by enhancing the path with its iterated integrals (the signature). It underpins the theory of regularity stru…

label: Scalar curvature
description: The scalar curvature $R = g^{ij}R_{ij}$ is the trace of the Ricci tensor, the simplest local curvature invariant of a Riemannian manifold. Positive scalar curvature obstructs certain topologies (Gromov–Lawson–Rosenberg conjecture) and is studied via the Dirac operator and surgery techniques.

label: Schur functor
description: A Schur functor $\mathbb{S}_\lambda$ associated to a Young diagram $\lambda$ is an endofunctor of $\mathbf{Vect}$ sending a vector space $V$ to the $\lambda$-isotypic component of $V^{\otimes |\lambda|}$ under the symmetric group action. Schur functors produce all polynomial representations of $GL(V…

label: Seiberg–Witten invariant
description: Seiberg–Witten invariants are smooth 4-manifold invariants defined by counting solutions to the Seiberg–Witten equations (a system coupling a spinor and a $U(1)$ connection). They are easier to compute than Donaldson invariants and equivalent to them for simply-connected 4-manifolds by Witten's conj…

label: Shimura variety
description: A Shimura variety is a complex algebraic variety $\mathrm{Sh}(G,X) = G(\mathbb{Q})\backslash X \times G(\mathbb{A}_f)/K$ defined by a reductive group $G$ over $\mathbb{Q}$ and a Hermitian symmetric domain $X$. Shimura varieties serve as moduli spaces for abelian varieties with additional structure a…

label: Singular integral
description: A singular integral is a convolution operator $Tf(x) = \mathrm{p.v.}\int K(x-y)f(y)\,dy$ where the kernel $K$ has a non-integrable singularity at the origin (e.g., the Hilbert transform, Riesz transforms). Calderón–Zygmund theory gives $L^p$ boundedness for $1 < p < \infty$ and weak $(1,1)$ estimate…

label: Singular space
description: A singular space is a topological or algebraic-geometric space that fails to be a manifold at some points (the singular locus). Analytic tools — intersection homology, perverse sheaves, resolution of singularities, Alexandrov geometry — extend manifold theory to this setting.

label: SLE (Schramm–Loewner evolution)
description: Schramm–Loewner evolution $\mathrm{SLE}_\kappa$ is a one-parameter family of random fractal planar curves characterised by conformal invariance and the domain Markov property. It describes the scaling limits of interfaces in critical 2D statistical mechanics models (percolation, Ising, loop-erased r…

label: Sobolev space
description: The Sobolev space $W^{k,p}(\Omega)$ consists of $L^p(\Omega)$ functions whose distributional derivatives up to order $k$ also lie in $L^p(\Omega)$, with norm $\|u\|_{W^{k,p}} = \sum_{|\alpha|\le k}\|D^\alpha u\|_p$. Sobolev embedding theorems control pointwise or Hölder regularity of functions in th…

label: Spectral gap
description: The spectral gap of a Markov semigroup or Laplacian is the difference $\lambda_1 - \lambda_0$ between the smallest two eigenvalues, controlling the rate of convergence to equilibrium. Positive spectral gap implies exponential mixing and is related to Poincaré inequalities and expander graphs.

label: Spectral sequence
description: A spectral sequence $\{E_r^{p,q}, d_r\}$ is a sequence of bigraded abelian groups with differentials $d_r\colon E_r^{p,q}\to E_r^{p+r,q-r+1}$ such that $E_{r+1} = H(E_r, d_r)$, converging (under suitable conditions) to the graded pieces of a filtered group. Spectral sequences compute cohomology of f…

label: Sphere spectrum
description: The sphere spectrum $\mathbb{S}$ is the unit object in the symmetric monoidal $\infty$-category of spectra; its homotopy groups $\pi_n(\mathbb{S})$ are the stable homotopy groups of spheres. It plays the role of $\mathbb{Z}$ in algebra: every ring spectrum is a module over $\mathbb{S}$.

label: Stable ∞-category
description: A stable $\infty$-category is an $\infty$-category with a zero object in which a square is a pushout if and only if it is a pullback, making the suspension functor $\Sigma$ an equivalence. The $\infty$-derived category and the $\infty$-category of spectra are the primary examples; they provide the s…

label: Stochastic PDE
description: A stochastic PDE (SPDE) is a partial differential equation driven by a random noise term, typically space-time white noise. Hairer's theory of regularity structures and Gubinelli–Imkeller–Perkowski's paracontrolled distributions provide rigorous solution theories for subcritical SPDEs like KPZ and $…

label: Surgery theory
description: Surgery theory classifies manifolds by cutting out embedded spheres and re-gluing to change topology, guided by the surgery obstruction in Wall's L-groups. Combined with the s-cobordism theorem, it gives a complete classification of simply-connected manifolds of dimension $\ge 5$ via the surgery exa…

label: Symmetric monoidal category
description: A symmetric monoidal category is a category $\mathcal{C}$ with a tensor product $\otimes$, unit object $I$, and natural isomorphisms for associativity, unit, and commutativity satisfying coherence axioms. Examples include $(\mathbf{Ab},\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}})$, $(\mathbf{Vect}_k,\otimes_k)$, and the $…

label: Teichmüller space
description: The Teichmüller space $\mathcal{T}_g$ parameterises marked hyperbolic metrics (equivalently, complex structures) on a genus-$g$ surface up to isotopy. It carries the Teichmüller metric (Finsler) and the Weil–Petersson metric (Kähler), and the mapping class group acts on it properly discontinuously.

label: Topological field theory
description: A topological field theory (TFT) is a symmetric monoidal functor $Z\colon \mathbf{Cob}_n \to \mathbf{Vect}$ from the cobordism category to vector spaces, assigning invariants to manifolds that depend only on topology. Atiyah's axioms formalise this; examples include Chern–Simons theory and Reshetikh…

label: Topological K-theory
description: Topological K-theory $K^0(X)$ is the Grothendieck group of (virtual) complex vector bundles on a compact Hausdorff space $X$; together with $K^1(X)$ it forms a generalised cohomology theory by Bott periodicity $K^n(X)\cong K^{n+2}(X)$. It is connected to the index theorem and C*-algebra K-theory via…

label: Topos theory
description: A Grothendieck topos is a category equivalent to the category of sheaves on a site; elementary toposes are categories satisfying Lawvere–Tierney axioms. Topos theory provides a generalised notion of space that encompasses algebraic geometry (étale topos), logic (Heyting algebra), and higher topos th…

label: Torsion
description: Algebraic torsion refers to elements $x$ in an abelian group with $nx=0$ for some $n>0$; Whitehead torsion $\tau(f)\in Wh(\pi_1)$ is an obstruction to a homotopy equivalence of CW complexes being a simple homotopy equivalence. Reidemeister torsion is a finer invariant distinguishing manifolds with t…

label: Trace map
description: Trace maps — including the Dennis trace $K_*(R)\to HH_*(R)$, the cyclotomic trace $K_*(R)\to TC_*(R)$, and the Chern character — relate algebraic K-theory to more computable invariants (Hochschild homology, topological cyclic homology). They are key tools for K-theory computations.

label: Unitary representation
description: A unitary representation of a group $G$ is a continuous group homomorphism $\pi\colon G \to U(\mathcal{H})$ for some Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}$. The unitary dual $\hat G$ classifies irreducible unitary representations; its structure governs harmonic analysis on $G$ via the Plancherel theorem.

label: Universal coefficient theorem
description: The universal coefficient theorem relates the homology $H_n(X;G)$ with coefficients in $G$ to $H_n(X;\mathbb{Z})$ and $H_{n-1}(X;\mathbb{Z})$ via a short exact sequence involving $\mathrm{Tor}$ and $\otimes G$. An analogous sequence computes cohomology from homology using $\mathrm{Ext}$ and $\mathrm…

label: Virtual fundamental class
description: The virtual fundamental class $[M]^{\mathrm{vir}} \in H_*(M)$ is a homology class associated to a moduli space $M$ of expected dimension $d$, defined using a perfect obstruction theory even when $M$ is not of pure dimension $d$. It is the correct cycle for integration in Gromov–Witten and Donaldson–…

label: Von Neumann algebra
description: A von Neumann algebra is a *-subalgebra of $B(\mathcal{H})$ that is closed in the weak operator topology, equivalently equal to its double commutant $M = M''$. Factors — von Neumann algebras with trivial centre — are classified into types I, II, III; the type $\mathrm{II}_1$ case is the setting of f…

label: Waldhausen K-theory
description: Waldhausen's algebraic K-theory $A(X)$ of a space $X$ is defined via the K-theory of the Waldhausen category of finite retractive spaces over $X$, with $A(*) \simeq K(\mathbb{Z})$ at a point. It appears in the stable parametrised h-cobordism theorem and connects to topological Hochschild homology vi…

label: Weil conjectures
description: The Weil conjectures (proved by Deligne in 1974) describe the zeta function $Z(X/\mathbb{F}_q; t)$ of a variety over a finite field: it is a rational function satisfying a functional equation, and its zeroes and poles lie on circles of prescribed radius (Riemann hypothesis analogue). Their proof use…

label: White noise
description: White noise is a generalised stochastic process $\xi$ formally satisfying $\mathbb{E}[\xi(x)\xi(y)] = \delta(x-y)$; rigorously it is a random tempered distribution. Space-time white noise drives SPDEs such as the stochastic heat equation; its regularity properties necessitate renormalisation in subc…

label: Whitehead group
description: The Whitehead group $Wh(G) = K_1(\mathbb{Z}[G])/\{\pm g : g\in G\}$ measures the obstruction to a homotopy equivalence of finite CW complexes being a simple homotopy equivalence (Whitehead torsion). The Farrell–Jones conjecture implies $Wh(G) = 0$ for torsion-free groups.

label: $\mathcal{Z}$-stability
description: A separable unital C*-algebra $A$ is $\mathcal{Z}$-stable if $A \cong A \otimes \mathcal{Z}$, where $\mathcal{Z}$ is the Jiang–Su algebra. $\mathcal{Z}$-stability is a regularity condition that, combined with finite nuclear dimension, implies classifiability by the Elliott invariant in the classific…

label: O1 — Push frontiers in K-theory and attack open questions in topology
description: This group builds and computes algebraic "fingerprints" of spaces and algebras. K-theory is the central fingerprint. They use it to decide when two shapes are really the same, to bound the curvature of manifolds, and to classify operator algebras.

label: O2 — Study moduli spaces to advance the Langlands programme and other fields
description: Instead of studying one curve or one geometric object, they study the *space of all of them*. The arithmetic of these spaces feeds the Langlands programme; their geometry feeds topology, differential geometry, and physics.

label: O3 — Open questions in C\*-algebras, model building, and set theory
description: This group builds different logical universes to test which mathematical statements are provable, independent, or require strong infinity assumptions — probing the boundaries of what mathematics can decide.

label: O4 — PDEs and differential geometry via method transfer
description: They ask: how much does *local* bending control the *overall* shape of a space? They aim to classify highly curved or Einstein spaces, gradually dropping symmetry assumptions until none remain.

label: O5 — Stochastic analysis for non-commutative field theories
description: They use randomness and non-commutative probability to rigorously *construct* quantum field theories on exotic "non-commutative" spaces — a goal physicists assume but mathematicians have not fully proven.

label: O6 — Structures and their limits in stochastic models
description: They study large random networks, tilings, and particle systems, and the smooth limiting shapes that emerge when these structures grow very large.

label: O7 — Structures in multiscale processes; effective models
description: Many systems mix small and large scales. They derive simpler "effective" equations valid at the large scale, prove how accurate these approximations are, and solve optimisation/control problems on top of them.

label: O8 — Innovative simulation via model- and data-based approaches
description: They put machine learning on a rigorous mathematical footing — analysing why training works and combining data-driven networks with classical models to build fast, *provably accurate* simulators for physics and engineering.

description: K-groups and Cohomology

label: Topic 2: Moduli Spaces in Arithmetic and Geometry
description: Moduli Spaces in Arithmetic and Geometry

label: Topic 3: Models and Universes
description: Models and Universes

description: Groups and Actions

description: Curvature, Shape and Global Analysis

label: Topic 6: Singularities and Partial Differential Equations
description: Singularities and Partial Differential Equations

label: Topic 7: Field Theory and Randomness
description: Field Theory and Randomness

label: Topic 8: Random Discrete Structures and Their Limits
description: Random Discrete Structures and Their Limits

description: Multiscale Processes and Effective Behaviour

label: Topic 10: Deep Learning and Surrogate Methods
description: Deep Learning and Surrogate Methods

label: Cohomology
description:
Imagine you want to tell shapes apart without touching them: a ball, a doughnut,
a pretzel. A doughnut has **one hole**, a pretzel has **two**. Cohomology is a
careful way of *counting holes of different kinds* (gaps you can loop a string
around, hollow cavities, etc.) and recording the answer as a …

label: K-theory (algebraic and topological)
description:
Think of all the ways you can lay down arrows (little vectors) smoothly over a
shape — for example combing the hair on a ball. Some arrangements can be undone
to nothing, others are "stuck". K-theory bundles up all these arrangements and
turns them into a number system you can add and subtract with.…

label: The Farrell–Jones conjecture
description:
Some mathematical objects attached to a group are very hard to compute directly.
This conjecture says: you can always build the hard global answer out of easy
local pieces — namely the small, simple subgroups. Like reconstructing a whole
mosaic if you know just a few special tiles.

label: The Borel conjecture and topological rigidity
description:
If two rubbery shapes have the *same kind of fundamental loops*, are they really
the same shape? For a special well-behaved family the answer is conjectured to be
yes — they cannot be secretly different.

label: C\*-algebras and their classification
description:
Quantum physics needs a number system where multiplication order matters
($`a\times b \ne b\times a`$). C\*-algebras are such systems of "operators".
K-theory gives each one a fingerprint; mathematicians want to prove that the
fingerprint alone tells them apart.

label: Moduli space
description:
Instead of studying one triangle, imagine a "map of all triangles", where each
point of the map stands for one possible triangle shape. Walking across the map
continuously morphs one triangle into another. A **moduli space** is exactly such
a "map of all shapes of a given type" — its points *are* ge…

label: The Langlands programme
description:
There are two very different worlds in mathematics: one about whole numbers and
equations, another about waves and symmetry. The Langlands programme is a giant
dictionary claiming these two worlds secretly say the same things — a "Rosetta
Stone" of mathematics.

label: Compactification and stratification
description:
A map of all shapes often has "edges" where shapes degenerate (a circle pinching
to a figure-eight). To study the map fully you add those boundary cases, like
adding the horizon to a landscape so nothing runs off the edge.

label: Cohomological invariants and intersection theory
description:
Once you have your map of shapes, you can ask geometric questions about the map
itself: how many curves pass through given points? Counting such things cleverly
reveals hidden patterns and even connects to physics.

label: Diffeomorphism groups and spaces of metrics
description:
Take a rubber shape; all the ways of smoothly stretching it without tearing form
their own "space of stretches". And all the ways of measuring distances on it
(its possible geometries) form yet another space. Studying these spaces tells us
which shapes are flexible and which are rigid.

label: Model theory and first-order structures
description:
A "rule book" (axioms) can be obeyed by different "worlds". For example "every
element has an opposite" is obeyed by the integers and by many other systems.
Model theory studies all the worlds that obey a given rule book, and what they
must have in common.

label: Homogeneous structures and Fraïssé limits
description:
Some objects look "the same from everywhere" — like an infinite perfectly regular
network where every point has an identical view. You can build such an object by
gluing together all its small finite pieces in the most generic way.

label: Universes of set theory and forcing
description:
All of mathematics can be built from "sets". But there is not just one possible
universe of sets — there are many, and some questions (like exactly how many real
numbers there are) get *different answers* in different universes. Set theorists
build and compare these universes.

label: Large cardinals
description:
Infinity comes in many sizes. "Large cardinals" are sizes of infinity so vast
that their mere existence cannot be proved from the usual rules — yet assuming
them settles many otherwise-undecidable questions far below.

label: The Axiom of Determinacy
description:
Imagine an infinite game where two players alternately pick numbers forever. One
of them is declared the winner by some rule. Determinacy says: in such games one
side always has a guaranteed winning strategy. Surprisingly, whether this holds
depends on which mathematical universe you live in.

label: Groups and group actions
description:
A **group** is the mathematics of symmetry: the collection of all moves that leave
something looking the same (rotating a square by 90°, flipping it). A group
**acts** on an object when each of its moves rearranges that object. Studying the
moves tells you about the object, and vice versa.

label: C\*-algebras, crossed products and Cartan subalgebras
description:
When a symmetry group acts on a space, you can package the whole "space + symmetry"
into a single algebraic object (a crossed product). Inside it sometimes hides a
simpler, well-organised "coordinate grid" called a Cartan subalgebra, which makes
the whole thing far easier to understand.

label: Amenability and property (T)
description:
Some groups are "easygoing" (you can average over them in a consistent way) and
some are "rigid" (they resist small deformations and approximations). These two
extremes behave very differently and lead to very different geometry.

label: Entropy and orbit equivalence
description:
Take a system that evolves by repeating a symmetry over and over. "Entropy"
measures how unpredictable or complex this evolution is. Two systems can look
different but secretly be re-labellings of each other — that is orbit equivalence.

label: Algebraic groups, Lie groups and Bruhat–Tits buildings
description:
Beyond finite symmetry there are *continuous* symmetries — like all rotations of a
sphere, which form a smooth, flowing family. These richer symmetry objects come
with their own geometric "skeletons" that help us understand them.

label: Riemannian manifolds
description:
The surface of the Earth is curved: triangles drawn on it have angles adding to
*more* than 180°. A **Riemannian manifold** is any smooth curved space — of any
dimension — where we can still measure lengths and angles, hence distances and
"straightest possible" paths.

label: Curvature and topological obstructions
description:
The amount and sign of curvature limits what shapes are possible. You cannot comb a
hairy ball flat, and you cannot wrap a sphere onto a doughnut without distortion —
curvature "knows" the global shape.

label: Ricci flow and geometric flows
description:
Imagine a lumpy ball of dough that automatically smooths itself out, the bumps
flowing away until it becomes a perfect sphere. **Ricci flow** is an equation that
makes a curved space evolve and smooth its curvature over time — the tool Perelman
used to prove the Poincaré conjecture.

label: Geodesics and infinite-dimensional shape spaces
description:
The shortest flight path between two cities curves across the globe — that is a
geodesic. Now imagine a "space" whose points are entire *shapes* (say, all possible
outlines of a hand). A geodesic there is the most efficient way to morph one shape
into another — useful in computer graphics and medica…

label: Index theory and scalar curvature
description:
There is a deep bridge between *analysis* (solving equations on a shape) and
*topology* (the shape's holes). Counting the solutions of a special equation
secretly counts holes — and this forbids certain shapes from being curved a
certain way.

label: Partial differential equations (PDEs)
description:
Most laws of nature say how things *change*: how heat spreads, how waves travel,
how planets pull. A PDE is an equation that relates these rates of change across
space and time. Solving it predicts the future from the present.

label: Singularities
description:
Sometimes a smooth process suddenly breaks down: a wave crests and shatters, a
droplet pinches off, gravity collapses matter into a black hole. These breakdown
points are **singularities** — and they are usually the most interesting and
hardest part to understand.

label: Einstein manifolds and general relativity
description:
Einstein discovered that gravity *is* the curving of spacetime by mass. The
equation governing this is one of the most important PDEs in physics, and the
shapes (geometries) it allows include black holes.

label: Black hole stability (the Kerr problem)
description:
If you "poke" a spinning black hole, does it wobble and settle back down, or fall
apart? Physically we expect it to settle — but proving this from the equations is
one of the great open challenges of mathematical physics.

label: Regularity theory and singular stochastic PDEs
description:
"Regularity" asks how smooth a solution is. When you add random noise everywhere
(like static), solutions become so jagged that the equation barely makes sense.
New mathematics was invented to give these noisy equations a precise meaning.

label: Quantum field theory (QFT)
description:
At the smallest scales, particles are ripples in invisible "fields" filling all of
space. Quantum field theory is the rulebook for these ripples — it powers the
Standard Model of particle physics. But making its mathematics fully rigorous is a
million-dollar open problem.

label: Stochastic quantisation
description:
Instead of building a field "all at once", let it wiggle randomly over time like a
jittering elastic sheet until it settles into a stable random pattern. That
equilibrium pattern *is* the quantum field. Randomness becomes a construction tool.

label: Non-commutative geometry and the Moyal space
description:
Normally $3\times 5 = 5\times 3$. But in quantum mechanics, measuring position then
momentum differs from the reverse order. "Non-commutative spaces" build geometry
out of such order-sensitive coordinates — a geometry where $xy \ne yx$.

label: Free probability and topological recursion
description:
Ordinary probability adds independent random numbers (giving the bell curve). When
the "random numbers" are huge matrices that don't commute, a new kind of
probability is needed — and counting the shapes (surfaces) that appear organises
the answers into an elegant recursive pattern.

label: Disorder, scaling limits and the polaron
description:
A single electron dragging a cloud of distortion through a crystal behaves like a
heavier "dressed" particle — a polaron. Zoom out from many small random effects and
clean, universal behaviour emerges. Studying these limits links physics and
probability.

label: Random graphs
description:
Draw some dots and connect each pair by flipping a coin. The result is a random
network — a toy model of a friendship network or the web. Surprisingly, as you add
dots, a giant connected cluster appears almost suddenly once links are frequent
enough.

label: Percolation and phase transitions
description:
Imagine randomly opening or blocking the tiny channels in a porous rock. Below a
critical fraction of open channels, water cannot cross; above it, it suddenly can.
That sharp switch is a phase transition — the same idea as water freezing.

label: Random tessellations and polytopes (stochastic geometry)
description:
Scatter points at random, then give each point the territory closest to it — you get
a random patchwork of cells, like cracked mud or a giraffe's coat. Mathematicians
measure the typical shapes, areas and number of sides of these random tiles.

label: Interacting particle systems and scaling limits
description:
Many simple agents following local random rules — cars on a road, particles hopping
on a line — together produce complex large-scale patterns. Zooming out turns the
jiggling crowd into a smooth flowing equation.

label: Fluctuations, disorder and universality
description:
Add many independent random things and you almost always get the same bell-shaped
curve — no matter the details. This "universality" is why averages are predictable.
Researchers hunt for which random systems share such universal large-scale laws.

label: Multiple scales and effective models
description:
A sponge looks solid from far away but is full of tiny holes up close. To predict
how it soaks up water you don't track every pore — you find an "effective" rule for
the whole sponge. Multiscale maths is the art of jumping from tiny details to
big-picture rules.

label: Homogenisation
description:
A finely striped material (alternating metal and plastic) behaves, overall, like a
single new material with averaged properties. Homogenisation computes those average
properties from the fine pattern.

label: Optimal control and the curse of dimensionality
description:
A pilot steering a rocket wants the *best* path — least fuel, fastest arrival. Find
the best control among infinitely many options. But when there are many knobs to
turn at once, the number of possibilities explodes — the "curse of dimensionality".

label: Model order reduction
description:
If you must re-run a slow, expensive simulation thousands of times (for different
settings), you first build a cheap "mini-model" that captures the essentials, then
run that instead. You trade a little accuracy for enormous speed.

label: Mixing, equilibration and kinetic equations
description:
Stir milk into coffee: streaks stretch and fold until the colour is uniform. How
fast can stirring mix things, and how fast does a gas settle to equilibrium? These
rates are what this unit pins down.

label: Artificial neural networks
description:
A neural network is a flexible mathematical "function machine": you feed it inputs,
it produces outputs, and by adjusting many internal dials you can make it imitate
almost any pattern from examples. It is the engine behind modern AI.

label: Stochastic gradient descent (and its analysis)
description:
To train the network you tweak its dials to reduce its mistakes, step by step,
always heading "downhill" on an error landscape. To go fast you estimate the
downhill direction from a small random handful of examples each step. That is
stochastic gradient descent.

label: Deep learning methods for (S)PDEs
description:
Classical methods solve a physics equation by chopping space into a fine grid —
impossible when there are too many dimensions. Instead, train a neural network to
*be* the solution. The equation itself becomes the teacher.

label: Surrogate models and multi-fidelity / certification
description:
Use a fast rough estimator most of the time and an expensive accurate one only
occasionally, then blend them so the final answer is both cheap *and* trustworthy.
A "certified" surrogate even tells you how wrong it might be.

label: Inverse problems
description:
A CT scanner never sees inside you directly — it measures shadows and reconstructs
the interior. Working backwards from effects to hidden causes is an inverse problem,
and small measurement errors can blow up, so it must be done carefully.
